speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like for you to tell us now when they began to form the ghetto in your community?

speaker: interviewee
text: When they formed the ghetto, they displaced the people where they were living. Around the city, closer into this. And they-- there wasn't walls like you had in Warsaw or Lodz. We had some wooden fences were made and certain entrances. I-- I used to go out every day because we went to work. So as a group, we went into the stone quarries or on the highways. When we went out by group, we led-- somebody took us to work. But come-- from coming home from work, we came on our own. You know, we came through back ways. And we always used to be able to buy something on the way-- and came in through back ways. We shouldn't be stopped by anybody, you know, to bring in some food for ourselves. People had to be crowded. In-- in one or two rooms, I remember, the Pole who used to live with us had to move out. And another Jewish family moved in in that one room there. But we had our own place. I know my brother worked for the-- there was a soup kitchen for the poor people that came in from out of town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he worked in that kitchen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: David. Because his friend used to be the president of the Judenrat--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you--

speaker: interviewee
text: --before the war, for the [INAUDIBLE], which was like a Jewish-- like here the Federation looked after [INAUDIBLE], paid their abonnement, paid their [INAUDIBLE], you know. And the upkeep of the synagogue, you were taxed so much a year. You know. So his brother used to be there. And they formed that kitchen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name? Your brother's friend.

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Margolis. ?] [? Joseph ?] [? Margolis ?] was his name. And they-- they also belonged to-- every morning, they learned chess in the [INAUDIBLE]. So you know, so that's how that formed that kitchen for the people to, you know, be able to get some food. The-- I mean, I can't say that the conditions-- people didn't starve from hunger in my hometown. Because it was able to-- food was able to be bought if there was money. The only thing is, I mean, you couldn't go. You couldn't earn any money. Because people tried to sell whatever they had, because there was no commerce except for people whatever-- that had some money. When the farmers came in, you were able to buy, you know, potatoes, and chicken, eggs, whatever it is. Naturally, the prices went up. And that's what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then a lot of people were sent away to labor camps. We had once they circled the city. And they grabbed every young man they could get a hold on. And at that time, they took away a ton of young people into the labor camps, into Skarzysko and Ch-- in-- the other one was-- Skarzysko and-- right nearby, there were two-- two ammunition factories. What's the name of the other place? Did they take anybody in your family at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. One of my brothers was taken, but he-- he came home a month later. He escaped. And he came home a month later from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Benjamin. He came home. You know, the Jewish police, whatever, in the ghetto, they actually did the searching. And they brought those people in. Skarzysko-Kamienna and Starachowice. That's where they took him. My brother was in Starachowice, and then he came back. And they also sent a lot of people into the East, further East, to labor camps. Some of them, you know, escaped and came back. And that's how we knew what was happening.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. This was the labor camps where they were formed. Then after the ghetto, you see, when the wyswietlenia was or the-- you know, the day when they sent everybody-- in the morning, they went around with loudspeakers and everybody should assemble at a certain point which was actually in the place where the farmers used to come and sell their horses and pigs.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we assembled there, everybody. I remember it was raining in the morning. When we got there, it was very cloudy. And then they went around, through the-- how do you call it? When people are assembled, you know, standing, you know, like in four, and five, and this-- and they picked out young men to step out. My brother and I-- Benjamin and myself-- we were picked out. And it was over 100 people that were left in the city.

speaker: interviewee
text: After they sent everybody away to Treblinka at that time-- they marched them away-- we had to clean up the city. The same night, we heard some screams. They discovered a hiding place where about 40 people. And they took those 40 people up to the cemetery, and they killed them. The next morning, we had to go and bury those people. A lot of them, what we knew, you know, who they were.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you bury them?

speaker: interviewee
text: We just dug a grave, a communal grave, and just dumped them in. And that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was on the grounds of the cemetery?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was on the grounds of the cemetery. They-- they marched them up to the cemetery. And that's where they killed them, in the front, you know, where you just enter. Not in cemetery, but where you just enter into the cemetery. So the next morning, we had to go there and to assemble, you know, and dug a grave, and put them in.

speaker: interviewer
text: When--

speaker: interviewee
text: And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, go on.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- we were-- and this was from the day when they sent everybody away until January the 10, I think. We worked, we were there, you know, to gathered a lot of stuff from the houses and the Germans sold it to the Poles and things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was 1942. Then in-- in end of December--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Before you go forward, I'd like to go back a little bit to when your family was still together, before the transport.

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the-- my father died in 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he die?

speaker: interviewee
text: He died of-- I mean, things were-- my brother was killed, so this took a toll off him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Moshe, which was in the Polish army. And he was 71 years old-- 72-- I think he was 71 or 72-- when he died. And this took a toll off him and the war with everything, so he was lucky that he died. We buried him the same day and were able to have services in the house for him and to say kaddish for him the whole year. Then, you know, when-- when this happened, after they sent everybody away, the only left at home was myself in the house, my other brother, Schmuel Meir, and Benjamin, and my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: What had happened to your mother and your other--

speaker: interviewee
text: My--my mother and my [INAUDIBLE] in the assembly place, the same as I was. And they were-- you know, after they picked us out, they remained there. And they went with the whole-- with all other peoples that were marched away in the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where they were going at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but we found out afterwards. A few days later, somebody escaped. And they said they took him to Treblinka and what's happening to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That they were killing them there, gassing them there. So after this happened, you know, we were left in [INAUDIBLE] for those-- I mean, November and December. And then the Germans announced they're forming a second ghetto in Sandomierz. And all the people that were hiding or there-- those whatever left in the cities for cleaning up this, they should assemble in Sandomierz. And this is going to be permanent for everybody to-- to live.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when you were cleaning out your town from the ghetto, what were you do-- were you literally cleaning out the houses of the people?

speaker: interviewee
text: You-- you took out if there was merchandise. I mean, you didn't take out the things, you know, like old furniture, things like that, only what they could get money for to, you know, to sell it to the Poles who could have money for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you try to keep any of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: We couldn't keep anything of it. Because the Germans were there. It wasn't just left-- they were always with us. They were guarding us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you living at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in three buildings. The-- we occupied three buildings. Nobody was there, in those buildings, so we just occupied. Everybody was in a room, and that was it. In one nearby, one right near the synagogue, three tall buildings what they belonged-- I remember even one belonged to the [? Steinman's ?] family. The other one belonged to the [? Lipman ?] family. And the third one, I think, was [? Weinberg. ?] Those three houses, you know, big-- I mean two-story homes what were living in there, those hundred people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were all men?

speaker: interviewee
text: Men and-- no, some women, too. There we some girls in there, too. Then when they send us to Sandomierz, I mean, the conditions were worse yet, because it was very crowded. It was a very small area that the ghetto was formed. And people came from all over, you know. Everybody believed maybe this-- this will be permanent. And in fact, I met a sister of mine, which was hiding out before. And she came in with her husband and their four children, came into Sandomierz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yittela with her husband [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] A [? brother-in-law ?] of theirs, which arrived while they were hiding, came and begged them to leave the ghetto to go with him, you know, into hiding.

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me, who was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: A brother-in-law of my sister came to the ghetto and asked them to leave the ghetto and go with them to-- to hide where they were. But they didn't. They thought that-- believed the Germans that it's going to be better this way. So they stayed in the-- in Sandomierz. And then, naturally, the 16 of January, 1943, you know, thing happened again. They assembled everybody in the center of the city for the [INAUDIBLE], what they called, you know, send them away. On the-- on the point there, they picked out 150 people, young men. I was between them. And my brother was in the group.

speaker: interviewer
text: Benjamin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Benjamin. And they send us to Skarzysko labor came. My brother, Schmuel Meir, he was in another camp in Sandomierz, in [INAUDIBLE], which he remained there. We went to Skarzysko. And was-- we were assigned to work, which was the better work. Because there were three camps-- A, B, and C. C, people were-- became yellow from working with the powders in the-- to the ammunition. And in work B, they made the big cannon thing. It was also very heavy work. And in this camp, you know, it was smaller. i mean, the factories were smaller parts of this performed. We-- we were taken to the place where the barracks were. And that's where they assigned, you know, places in the barrack. And then we were assigned to which place you were going to go and work. You walked with the work Werkschutz on both side of you. You know, because it was half a mile when you walked into the ammunition factory. And you were assigned to this. And we worked in three shifts. Every week, we had a different shift-- like from 8:00 till 4:00, or from 4:00 to 11:00, and then from 11:00 til the morning. That's how we were working. And the life was tough, because if you didn't have no means, you had to live on the-- on the food what they gave you. They gave you a soup. And they gave you, you know, piece of bread and coffee twice a day. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My brother and I lived in-- we had a little [INAUDIBLE] in the same [INAUDIBLE]-- you know, there were bunk beds like, you know, [INAUDIBLE] just boards, you know. And most of the people in the same barrack were from my-- were from my hometown and from another town, from Zwolen. You know, and we went every day to work, came back. And we worked like that. A lot of people died from hunger, typhus. I mean, I, myself, and my brother were stricken with typhus. But we had two girls from our hometown what they cooked for us and they nursed us through that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: The both of them are in To-- in Toronto. I don't remember their family names now, you know. Because they're married, and they have their own children. But both of them live in Toronto.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names then?

speaker: interviewee
text: One was [INAUDIBLE] and one was-- the other one was-- I can't even remember her name. They knew us from home. I mean, we knew them from home. Because we were neighbors. The city wasn't that big. And they nursed us through. And we were in Skarzysko till 1944, end of the summer '44. When the Russians started approaching closer by, they took us into the boxcars, and they transported us to Czestochowa. In Czestochowa, we worked for the same company, for HASAG. It was the same. They had factories there. And they-- some of the-- the people, when we came there, were shipped directly to Leipzig, Germany to factories. And some of them stayed there. We were there. And-- and one day, they came and they said that if somebody's got friends or relatives on another camp in the same city-- we were in the [INAUDIBLE]. And it so happened that my brother from Sandomierz-- in Liceum, from that camp-- was shipped to Czestochowa to the Czestochowianka, it was. So we changed and went over to be together with our third brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which brother was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was Schmuel Meir. And we moved over to that place. It was a new camp they had just built for repairing tanks and things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was its name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Czestochowianka, like the same name as the city. But, you know, it's-- like you would say Los Angelinos or something like that. That's the way Czestochowa and Czestochowianka-- that was the name of the camp. And we were there, in Czestochowianka, til about January the 16, I think, when the Russians were over bombarding the city. That's when they marched us out of camp to the train and put us into boxcars and shipped to Germany. It took us three days to arrive in Buchenwald.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where you were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know where we were going. We just-- we just-- while we were still at the station, we could hear the explosions from the-- from the-- what the Russians were bombarding the city. But, you know, we were locked up. We couldn't go. And I mean, the SS was around the-- the wagons. And they shipped us. We arrived, I think, January 16, 1945. We arrived in Buchenwald.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we arrived there, first, we were kept outside. And we had to go through, like-- through the showers. You know, leave our clothes. Gave us new clothes, because you shouldn't be able to smuggle anything in with you in case you had some money or things like that. Gave us clothes. The clothes didn't fit. I mean, I remember I had a pair of wooden Holland shoes, you know, like the-- like the Dutch people wear-- no socks, a pair of pants, no underwear, and a shirt, and a jacket. And then we had to stay and appeal. And the appeal was like 10 people deep. You know, they should-- every 10 had to step out, so they knew that's 100. That's another 100. I remember I was one who stepped out in the front of the 10, because I was in the front row. And that capo, whatever he was, when he came close to me, he looked at me, he said, [SPEAKING GERMAN] on my face. You know, because I had still colors on my face and this.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does that mean, what he said?

speaker: interviewee
text: That you still have your mother's milk on your cheeks. And he just gave me two back fives, just like that, for nothing. Then we were taken, you know, by groups to different barracks. I was-- we were assigned to barrack 52 in camp in Buchenwald.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were still with both of your brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was still with both of my brothers. We-- every day, they-- they took people to send them-- it was a-- like a transition period at that time. And they sent them to different places to go to work. The only-- the way they did it was when you went, you had like a little medallion to go and have your meal. And when-- when you went into the eating area, you gave that medallion. And you walked out-- after you finished, you walked out the other way. That's where they waited, on the other side, for you. And they grabbed you to send away on different transports. We were there til the last days, til they actually, like assembled people to walk at the [INAUDIBLE] march. Because they didn't have enough time to put you on trains, so they marched people. And there was the [INAUDIBLE] where they-- after they grabbed us there after the meal, they took us in like in a holding place. And from there, they gave you a soup, and a piece of bread. And they put you in a column to march out. Three times, we were able to escape back into the-- from the [INAUDIBLE] into the camp-- back into the camp. Imagine.

speaker: interviewer
text: How you do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were hiding there till later. Nobody was there. We went back to the camp. Next morning, all over same thing. You know, we waited. Because you wanted to eat. You couldn't go without food. We get one soup a day. We had a piece of bread and twice a day coffee. So we were lucky. We were able to-- three times, we were able to-- The last day, when we came back to camp, we went down to this children's barracks. And we hid under the barrack there. And we heard the-- the bombardment already about 11 o'clock in the morning. That's when the Americans came in. That was the first camp that was liberated by the Americans. It was April the 11, 1945. Thousands of people died afterwards with kindness, because there was so much food given to the people. And they couldn't cope. Their stomachs couldn't cope with all that fat food and all the things. And they died of dysentery. And you looked at the people, I mean, it was mussulmens. I mean, you can only hold on for so long. And then you just give up, your hope. Doesn't work like a machine. You can only go so far. And that's-- that's what it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like when the Americans came in? How did it feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Americans came in. I mean, it was euphoria that you survived. You looked around you, and you saw what happened. Because while you're there, you're not thinking of anything else. But you're thinking of food. Here, you ready-- the food question wasn't the problem. Because right away, you know, there was food. So you looked-- you realized what's happening. Because your mind, your eyes, saw something else. And you saw before you were just worried about yourself to survive, not get beaten up, not go someplace where you'll be taken away or have something done to you. But afterwards, you realized what had happened. I remember, the two days later when they brought in the Germans from Weimar, because Weimar was only 8 kilometers away, to show them what happened and they say-- them saying, you know, we didn't know nothing. I mean, Buchenwald had a crematorium. But it was only for dead people. It wasn't like live people to be burned, like Auschwitz or Birkenau or some place else. So the only time where the-- they kept us busy was they took us a couple of times to Weimar before the liberation to clean the city, you know, after bombardments. We were there in the city while bombs were falling in Weimar. And twice, they did that-- I was there. You know, and you approach the Buchenwald gate, you see [SPEAKING GERMAN]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what does that mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: That work makes you happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, the Americans took you to have you clean up from the bombs?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was before the Americans came.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans, yeah. [INAUDIBLE] the Germans. When the American came, we when ahead-- you know, a bunch of us went ahead and took over the [INAUDIBLE] where the SS used to stay. And about a few days later, the American wanted to take that over. And we had a confrontation for about two days. And we went on a hunger-- they wouldn't let any food to us. They wanted to take over the [INAUDIBLE]. They wanted us back to the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So naturally, we had to give in. If you don't get any food, you can't fight. [LAUGHS] You can't fight. So we had to give up and go back to the camp. But it didn't-- wasn't for long. Because, you know, the-- the agreement between the Americans and the English and the Russians was they were supposed to take over that part of Germany. So whoever didn't want to go back to Poland-- because there were a lot of French, Russians in the camp, the labor camp, and the Buchenwald camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were French, Hungarians. Whoever didn't want to go back to their own country, you know, repatriated had a choice to go into the English zone. And that's when we were transported to Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We're going to change the tape.
